replies.author: @castlerock58
replies.text:
@jameseckersley4926 
 Deterrence has already failed if a nuke is launched.  A single nuke hitting a city would have zero effect on US nukes or US command and control unless that city is Washington.  There is time to figure out where it came from and who the retaliate against. A single nuke on Chicago would leave the US a fully functioning country. A nuclear exchange with Russia would kill nearly every man, woman and child in the US and cause a nuclear winter that would kill five billion.

 A nuclear exchange with China would cost the US its 200 largest cities and power grid. It would also cause nuclear winter  The US would not survive as a country.  The US  could probably take out North Korea's ability to fire more nukes if they were the guilty party. 

In 1983, the Soviets detected 10 US ICBMs heading for their country. The officer resonsible for launching an all out Soviet Response, defied his orders because he did not think launching just 10 missiles made sense. He waited for confirmation and it turned out to be a glitch with the Soviet early warning system. A single ICBM makes even less sense than 10. An accident would be the most probable situation. There would be no time pressure to figure out what happened.  The priority would be to avoid accidental nuclear war with Russia and China since that would end the US.  

Not taking the call from the Russian leader would be insane. The leaders would be likely to try and save their countries like they did in intelligent movies like "Dr. Strangelove" and "Failsafe"  "Failsafe" deals with the US accidentally nuking Moscow and the negotiations to avoid a nuclear exchange that would destroy both countries. This movie is moronic since it does not give a realistic depiction of either an accidental nuclear launch or any rational attack strategy by any nuclear power. Not even North Korea.  If North Korea launched by accident, Kim Jong Un's priority would be to survive. 

Nukes are for deterring an attack. Diplomacy would need to prevent an accidental launch from leading to nuclear war. In the event of a probable accident, diplomacy would be the top priority. Chicago would be toast but if no other nukes are launched, you are not under time pressure to figure out what happened and get all nuclear powers to stand down so the US and civilization survives.

replies.author: @christoph4977
replies.text: @georgebailey8179 I was commenting on Critical Drinker's misunderstanding, not yours. His constant hammering about "blue-balling the audience", "not delivering" and pointing the critique at the wrong things like not delivering a conclusion. You critique a movie for an open ending? Really??? It's almost like all those 1-star IMDB reviews that literally expected Michael Bay style explosions and critisize the movie for not delivering something, that it never intended to. The movie's message? I think this should be pretty clear. You describet part of it yourself and it differs from what CD apparently saw. Additionally, I think the message the movie is _trying_ to convey is, that the USA is very badly prepared for a nuclear threat, that there are flawed persons involved in decisions, that have global repercussions, that in such a scenario, many things could go wrong and that yes, the best cause is "not to play the game" (if you get the quote). And in this, the movie fails on many fronts. I was not trying to defend the movie, only critisizing CD's approach. I have serious gripes with the movie starting with the very improbable scenario of not knowing the origins of the ICBM, only launching 2 interceptors and suggesting there would be no other means, the military leadership AGAIN portrayed as total baffoons who froth at their mouth at the opportunityty to start WW3 (without even knowing WHOM to hit), the lack of communication behind the scenes, the president not even brought to Air Force one and relying on one Lt. suggestions and many, many more. Those would be valid critiques of the movie IMO. And I am neither American, nor a defender of the US policy or military. Far from it.

replies.text:
I was fine with the ending remaining ambiguous about who was behind it and what happened. It was indeed stylistically designed to be that way, and just because that's a meme saying doesn't mean that artistic choices don't have a meaning, even if you can't really comprehend it. And if you "just wanted to watch a movie," then just put on American Pie or something bro, I don't know. Or any 80s action movie. But giving a clear answer like "Oh it was the North Koreans" in this movie would just have been dumb and would not have fit the tone the movie was going for in my book. It's possible it could have been done well enough, but I certainly don't mind the choice to leave it open-ended.

The problem with the movie was not the ambiguous ending, the problem was the completely unnecessary repetition of the plot. The first part was the best both writing and acting wise, it had the best character moments, and it had ALL of the tension. It already revealed exactly everything that was happening, which meant that the following two repetitions of the plot had absolutely nothing new to offer except slight character details, which given the context of the thing that we already know is happening, feels less impactful now.

replies.author: @rghm
replies.text:
That's actually a very good take on this film, @jontan6434 !  In Beckett's play, Godot is, of course, the MacGuffin. The play has nothing to do with Godot. Iinstead, it's all about the two main characters, Vladimir & Estragon, who are stand-ins, in Beckett's view, for contemporary human experience.  Likewise, in this film, the ICBM missile is a MacGuffin.  The story is not about the missile itself, but rather about the characters, mere humans, trying to cope with an existential dilemma.  Thinking that the film is about the missile, and the success or failure of the collective effort to stop it, is to miss the film's point.  Instead, this film is asking the viewers to ask questions, to put ourselves in those insane positions, and, finally, hopefully, to ponder, on our own, the conditions that have been created that might eventually lead us to those positions.

As for the reviewer's claim that each retelling section ("Hitting a Bullet with a Bullet" and "A House Filled with Dynamite") adds nothing new - as he puts it:  "None of these different perspectives added to my understanding of what was happening or reframe previously unexplained events in a new context to change my perspective on characters and their decisions or even just gave me something new and interesting to think about" - perhaps he wasn't paying full attention when he watched? 

Because, it's fairly clear that each of the three sections progress up the chain of command - tracing the actions from the soldiers at Ft. Greeley AK and the front line triage of the Situation Room in the first section, then higher up to the U.S. Strategic Command Center and the Presidential Emergency Operations Center in the second section, and finally to the highest levels, the Secretary of Defense and the President in the third section.  Yes, we see snippets of each of these locales and functionaries in all three sections. But only as the viewers finally reach the third section are we able to piece together what, from the point of views we've seen in the previous sections, were only fragments of the whole picture.

This stitching together of fragments of a larger picture as seen from various functionaries  argues against the reviewer's claim that "none of these different perspectives" add to an "understanding of what was happening or reframe previously unexplained events in a new context ."   In fact, I'd say that one of the points exceedingly well demonstrated by the film is the overarching fragmentary nature of information in such situations, where precise and complete information is so essential. Essential, yes - without a doubt. But in real world circumstances? Highly unlikely to be attainable.  And thereby hangs a tale. And, if you will, one of the morals of the story.

replies.author: @hagerty1952
replies.text:
Except for all-solid-fuel missiles like Trident or Minuteman. When you light one of those off, they have to burn to completion, no matter how near or far the target. To compensate for closer targets, they launch mostly straight up before transitioning to a ballistic trajectory, so if you were to backtrack the ballistic trajectory after burnout, it would be thousands of miles off. Additionally, Trident (I don't know about Minuteman) does a maneuver called "GEMS" for Generalized Energy Management System where the missile zig-zags on the way up to burn off even more fuel for close targets. That's the reason Trident has a minimum range as well as a maximum. Finally, once the bus is released (the part of the missile that carries the warheads) it starts maneuvering all over the sky to release each RB to its intended target.

Bottom line, we can track meteors hundreds of millions of miles back to their parent comet, but unless you're tracking a V2 with a non-detachable warhead, then it's really impossible to say with any certainty where it came from if you encounter it late in the flight. However, there is a network of monitoring satellites specifically tasked with recognizing rocket launches, and can see a booster heat signature within a few seconds of ignition. That reality sort of removes this whole plot point.

By that statement you confess that you have never worked in a role that required a security clearance. Just another anonymous internet "expert" who has no education or experience on the subject they are trying to pass themselves off as experts in. 

"Classified" is a category of information not a clearance level. No one in the history of the US has ever held a "classified" clearance level. You may be conflating the term "Confidential", which is a clearance level but it wouldn't even get you access to things like training manuals. Unrated deck seamen in the Navy have at least a "Secret" clearance which is the next step up from Confidential. After Secret is Top Secret, which sounds like it is everything but TS is simply the highest administrative clearance and in the grand scheme of things doesn't get you much. To start to get into real classified information, you have to get Sensitive Compartmented Information (SCI) clearances and things go up from there. If you don't have at least SCI, you don't really have a security clearance. And then of course there is the authentication factor of "Need to Know" which is a whole other discussion.

author: @jeremyevans8374
text: I think I understand the point. The point is to expose you to the weaknesses of our deterrent system, the limitations of our technology, and to force you to confront the nature of the mutually assured destruction hypothesis in all of its absurdity. In terms of that particular objective, showing what actually ended up happening is not necessary and possibly even counterproductive to the point that she is making which is the scale of guesswork involved in formulating a final response to such a threat. The point is there's no way of knowing what the outcome is going to be. So the director ditches the final outcome to drive that point home. That being said, it doesn't make for a very satisfying storytelling AT ALL. I wouldn't even classify it as entertainment. I think maybe the director sees it more as your civic duty to experience (kind of like Killers of the Flower Moon 😫).

text:
Watched this last night. The first part was GREAT. It was really fantastic. The small character moments, the acting, the building tension, it was all just amazingly done, and I was at the edge of my seat. Aaaaaand then the rest happened - and the new perspectives gave us nearly nothing new. The Secretary of Defense had a wife once and now has a daughter, but we already heard of his daughter who lives in Chicago in the first run-through of the scenario, now we just found out that they're sort of estranged, and we get him talking to her sadly not wanting to tell her what's about to happen, which is just a watered down version of the soldier of the first act talking to his mother, doing the exact same thing with talking sadly to her and not wanting to warn her.

And again the third adds little else except a President who does some unrelated stuff and has understandable anxiety over a decision that might lead to the end of the world, although he insists on talking to completely irrelevant people while ignoring the staff of expertise at his hands.

It's a shame, because the first section was really, really good. But all the great and powerful moments of the movie were all packed into it, and then we just got two worse repetitions of the same thing.

author: @weatherman667
text:
Fun fact:  Missile launches have giant plumes of fire and smoke that can stretch for kilometres.  Which means it's much easier to track a ballistic missile when it launches then at any other point in it's trajectory.  And this has been the case since the 60's.  The US has a large naval and airforce presence in Japan, largely to keep a watch out for ballistic missiles launched from North Korea and China.
The idea of not knowing where it came from is almost comical.
Not knowing does allow them to have a shocking reveal, with tense moments as they decide whether to retaliate or not... if they actually did that.  So, it would make sense from a storytelling perspective as a way to maintain the drama.  If it actually went to a climax and then denouement.

replies.text:
I was thinking the same thing. The nuclear powers are all being observed obsessively in order to get ahead of any launch. When North Korea prepares for a test launch, there is base activity, fueling, and other signs long before the actual launch takes place. We'd know where this missile comes from because it's our JOB to know. We know if they have subs capable of launching such a weapon. 

Another commenter pointed out that we detected the implosion of the Titan using standard aquatic listening procedures. You think we wouldn't hear an ICBM being launched, know exactly WHERE it was launched from, and do the backwards math?

And while our adversaries might take advantage of the impending destruction of a major American city, they'd probably NOT make any moves that would trigger a retaliation. When America gets in a mood, they become a nation that shoots first and asks questions maybe later.

replies.author: @musicismyyydrug
replies.text:
People who mock A House of Dynamite for not having a clean ending completely miss the genius of what it accomplishes. The film traps you inside the unthinkable, where the world teeters on the edge and no one truly knows what’s real or what’s coming. It builds unbearable tension, then refuses to let you escape it, because that’s exactly how it would feel in those moments.

Every quiet pause, every unanswered question, every face staring into the unknown is part of the masterpiece. It’s not about explosions or closure, it’s about the crushing weight of responsibility and the terror of uncertainty. 

Ask yourself, what would you do in that situation? And can you see just how much the human factor affects the built-up system we have created for ourselves? The nerves. The immediate abandonment of policies, such as everyone going and grabbing their personal cell phones and bringing them into classified workspaces so they can get one last call out to their loved ones. 

You owe yourself the experience of watching this movie.

replies.author: @markkirby9531
replies.text:
@Kreeos Precisely, they are United States, Russia, the United Kingdom, France, China, and India.
I would think you can rule out the UK & France, at least in today's world.

I doubt India would do such a thing, what could they possibly gain from doing so?

China and Russia?  Yeah, you can see how the present foreign policy situation could present a plausible scenario for them to do so.

But that raises two obvious questions.

I would think that both countries would have some contingencies in place in the event that their role is discovered.

Those contingencies are likely to be something that can be detected by various intelligence capabilities and/or assets. 

Second, I'd be willing to bet that there is an identified "fingerprint" for every single ballistic missile for every single country and system.

That is, there is some aspect of the booster, performance, etc. that is documented and can be used to identify any given launch.

Simple things like the acceleration rate is tied to the  thrust of the booster and the payload weight on board.

Typical burn times for any individual stage, assuming multiple stages, is another parameter.

Let's just chalk this up to another, as the Drinker is fond of saying, "something that is needed so the plot can happen."

replies.author: @ObssesedNuker
replies.text:
​ @Kreeos I mean, given it's launched from the Western Pacific, yes, it narrows it down to three suspects (North Korea, Russia, and China all have ballistic missile submarines, all could theoretically slip on into position somewhere in the Western Pacific, though only the Chinese and Russian ones IRL have the range for this: North Korea's sea-based missiles don't have that reach, they are working towards it and the movie implies they had achieved it at some point in the stories recent past). But that doesn't resolve the dilemma as it still leaves the question of "which of these three do we retaliate against? Do we rush the decision or should we hold back until we have a more solid picture?" knowing that getting it wrong carries both strategic and moral risks. The fundamental dilemma remains and the reason why the movie doesn't resolve the dilemma - whether it was a good idea to come down on a conclusive decision or not from a story telling perspective - is because what decision one takes is beside the actual point of the movie: to even be in such a situation is a strategic failure. And keep in mind, all the other countries strategic nuclear forces are also on high alert and the perpetrator would undoubtedly undertake a "launch on warning" the moment they caught the US retaliation heading their way, so even then the most likely outcome of even getting it right is mutual suicide. So once again, the situation is strategic insanity (something the movie acknowledges)... but a situation being "insane" is not the same as the situation being "unreal".

Of course, this also isn't actually a new message. The paradoxes and absurdities of nuclear weapons apparatus and the geopolitical implications surrounding them have been acknowledged in movies since Doctor Strangelove. The absurdities do have new twists - such as the complication each additional nuclear power adds to the global world compared to the Cold War, but many of their cores remain.

author: @DeletedReality2025
text: Maybe I missed it, because I was constantly hitting "pause" to look up at the ceiling and go "Why is everyone in this movie acting like they have no fucking idea what to do?" before I gritted my teeth and hit play again, but did they ever CONFIRM that A) the warhead actually did hit Chicago and B) that it detonated?  I don't think they did, so "maybe" the point of this was to show that someone "spoofing" our defense system could throw the whole chain of command into utter chaos and that we struck back blindly.  I was "suspicious" from the start when our satellites "missed" the launch itself.  HIGHLY unlikely, but "okay".  Then we failed to intercept the missile with our ABM's, which is more likely to happen, but it still lent itself to the question, did the 1st ABM "fail" or did it fail to lock on, because there was nothing for it to track, and did the 2nd ABM "miss" because again, it made the perfect intercept on the right vector, but when it got there, there was nothing to intercept?  Then suddenly we are in a rush to strike back because.............reasons?  Even if Chicago did get vaporized by a multi-megaton warhead, it impacts our ability to strike back how exactly?  If it was a sub orbital EMP attack over the central US, THEN it might make sense to strike back BEFORE detonation, because the EMP would seriously degrade our communications, could damage the circuits in our land based missiles and bombers, and etc.  But   to say we have to launch before we are even sure that there has been a detonation is beyond absurd, because that guarantees a global nuclear holocaust.  Anyone who thinks that every nuclear power on the planet wouldn't have their "finger on the button" once it was known that the US was facing a nuclear attack, is insane.  Nuclear weapons are "use 'em or lose 'em" once the shooting starts, and the SECOND the US activated the SIOP for a massive counter strike every country that had nukes is firing theirs off because the US obviously had NO idea where the alleged weapon came from, so the only logical conclusion would be that if we were emptying our silos, had our B-2's and B-52's in the air and our subs were sliding into firing position that we are hitting EVERYONE, which means it would be in the best interests of our "allies" like Britain, France and Israel to hit every other "unfriendly" nuclear power within reach as hard and fast as they could, and our "enemies" (Russia, China, N. Korea) would reach the same conclusion, with states like India and Pakistan joining in because if the whole world is going to go "boom" they want to do as much damage as THEY can as well to their regional rivals. author: @nathandenny6916
text: To the Drinker’s point about everyone knowing nuclear war is bad — I’m just old enough to remember how tense things were in the early–mid ’80s. The generation before mine did “duck and cover” drills, and even in college I’d still see buildings marked as civil defense shelters. But younger people, especially in the U.S., rarely think about this stuff. To them, nukes are more like movie props than real weapons. So I think films like this serve a purpose — they remind us that nuclear weapons still exist, they can be used, and huge resources are spent trying to keep that from ever happening.

In reality, Dr Strangelove was a wonderful satire by a great director but in drama, gravitas and social commentary it squashed Strangelove like a bug. author: @TheManoDestra
text:
Good things: as a guy who's Dad actually worked in Nuclear Clmmand and Control, it was fascinating to see how all the scenarios and characters worked things out. 

The whole point is to show how little control we really have when in comes to nuclear weapons and the comedy of errors that can take place. 

I know some folks are like "they just repeated it 3 times" but the point was to show the 4 distinct important parts of the NCC structure. 

That being said, I wish there was more to the movie, and I think they could have done more - but as someone who lived on an airbase that was targeted by nuclear missles,  it resonated with me. 

Not great, but not terrible.

author: @RaikeJansen
text: Another thing worth mentioning about these types of movies is that no country in the world actually has such advanced military command centers or the kind of seamless coordination they usually portray. Real operations are far more complex and limited than what we see on screen. In reality, the most sophisticated systems and best-trained crews are typically found in the Navy, especially aboard submarines, but even there, things are nowhere near as slick or flawless as movies suggest. Those with military training know this, and we often can’t help but laugh at how exaggerated and unrealistic most of these depictions are.

author: @Cosmic_Yak
text:
Guess I have to watch it now *sigh  I just can't wrap my head around it lol.  The presented fact they don't know where it came from, is mind blowing tbh.  I was a soldier in the U.S. Army and at a basic level of training, we can tell from an impact crater, tell the trajectory, speed, and size of munitions.  

Our F.O.'s (Foward Observers) can triangulate a missle, rocket, and even projo's mid flight right back to start points.  On the fly, and w/o a need for education in math beyond a pre-algebra level.  I dare say, in such cases (of pre-algebra), f.o.'s might even not realize their training is actually trig.  Never been an f.o., but have heard them in action over secured radio.  However, my gut says it might be true because in  my field we used terms like azimuth and back-azimuth, section and re-section, etc.  (i.e. learning math like equation w.o. actually calling it math).

Our field radars and their operators... I had the pleasure to be on a detail for them during a live fire exercise.  So impressive, just at that level. These folks could tell the difference from a bird and a missle, or even a Kevlar helmet tossed across in front of the machine.  The last bit I may or may not of as a gag and test, arrange to occur whilst I observed them observing  that.  The troop went hmmm, punched a bunch of keys...the map on rollers zipped and stopped.  A light projected on the map.  More keys clicked and the light dot upon another spot.  I asked what's that and he said... "probably a Kevlar helmet being tossed.  How? Dunno, but he was 100% correct.

Which is a long way saying... I can't imagine at the top level they can't figure out where it came from and couldn't make a fairly educated guess as to who.  Even a submarine launch!!!! Would still be telling a lot as to who.  A stealth drone traveling at mach 12 launching it would tell folks at that level, it was a Darpa tech and thusly the U.S. involvement for a hypothetical example.

The viewer was given all the perspectives, and an outline of the various options, leaving the viewer with the question, what would YOU do? None of the options were good.
